trajectory_summary: The call opened on a warm, cooperative customer and ended with a booked appointment — the structural outcome is a win. But the rep left meaningful energy on the table: the EV enthusiasm signal was acknowledged and then immediately redirected into a personal tangent, the Sunrun price anchor was never engaged, and two product knowledge gaps introduced uncertainty the closer will have to clean up. The appointment is real but the closer inherits an unchallenged competitor benchmark and a customer whose strongest buying signal (EV load, long-held aspiration) was never anchored.
outcome_dispute: Appointment booked — outcome tag of 'unknown' should be reclassified as 'booked.' The call ends with a confirmed date (May 8, 3PM), a text follow-up promised, and the customer agreeing to send the bill. The booking is real. Structural fragility exists on two dimensions: (1) bill photo not yet received — if this is a paid lead, the sit cannot be confirmed until the bill is captured; if organic, the bill is preferred but not required. Lead type is unknown from the transcript, so this fragility is conditional. (2) The Sunrun quote is the unchallenged benchmark going into the closer appointment.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill anchor depth — capture without flip
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I think the average that I've seen is between 1,100 and 1,400 around that range per month because I do have an EV as well.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The customer handed the rep the bill number and the EV load signal in the same breath — the two inputs that make the solar math most compelling. The rep acknowledged both and moved on without flipping either into emotional alignment against the utility. This is the codex's universal coaching gap (bill_anchoring 'capture + flip' pattern) showing up on a call where the customer was actively offering the leverage. The rep is likable and booked the appointment; the coaching is about converting that warmth into a stronger handoff, not fixing a broken call.

key_moments.quote: No. I just I thought about it because I was always thinking about getting solar, but I was always in an apartment. So Yeah. You know, this is the first time I've owned a house.
key_moments.stewart_read: Customer reveals a long-held aspiration for solar — this isn't casual curiosity, it's deferred intent finally becoming actionable. The codex flags interest_in_solar as 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' and calls for 60+ seconds of depth. Rep responded with a brief validation and moved immediately to the credit qualifier. The emotional weight of 'I always wanted this but couldn't' was available to anchor the entire pitch and was left untouched.

key_moments.quote: For any of the programs or incentives going on right now, you just need to have a credit score above 670. I'm assuming that's the case for you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep frames the credit qualifier as an assumption ('I'm assuming that's the case for you?') rather than a direct ask. Customer confirms with 'Yeah. Yeah.' — the qualifier is technically captured. However, the assumption framing skips the script's template entirely and may be papering over a real qualification check. Per the codex's unresolved policy question, it's unclear whether setter DQ on stated credit is even the right procedure — but the assumption framing means the rep didn't actually ask, they led the witness.

key_moments.quote: I think the average that I've seen is between 1,100 and 1,400 around that range per month because I do have an EV as well.
key_moments.stewart_read: Customer volunteers an EV — a high-load signal that makes this lead materially more valuable. The 1,100–1,400 kWh range is already above average; the EV explains why and signals the load will stay elevated. Rep acknowledged the EV ('Okay. You got an EV. That's good to know') but immediately pivoted to a personal EV conversation rather than anchoring the load implication to bill pain. The bill anchor was captured as data and dropped — the codex's 'capture + flip' pattern was not executed.

key_moments.quote: Alright. And do you like your EV? I've been thinking of getting one, but I just I don't know.
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep pivots from a high-value qualification signal (EV load) to a personal question about their own car-buying deliberation. This is not rapport-building in service of the call — it's scope drift that burns ~90 seconds and pulls the customer into an advisory role. The customer obliges with a detailed Tesla recommendation (04:37–05:20), which is warm but off-track. The call recovers, but the bill anchor window closes without a flip.
